title: Dynamical Asymmetry Is C1-Typical
speaker: Amie Wilkinson
abstract: I will discuss a result with Bonatti and Crovisier from 2009 showing that the C1 generic diffeomorphism f of a closed manifold has trivial centralizer; i.e. fg = gf implies that g is a power of f. I’ll discuss features of the C1 topology that enable our proof (the analogous statement is open in general in the Cr topology, for r greater than 1). I’ll also discuss some features of the proof and some recent work, joint with Danijela Damjanovic and Disheng Xu that attempts to tackle the non-generic case.

proof_stages.label: Baire-generic C1 perturbative framework
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets Diff^r(M) as a Baire topological group, explains residual genericity and the dependence on r, and singles out C1 because affine approximation and scale invariance make small local perturbations flexible.

proof_stages.label: Generic periodic and nonwandering input
proof_stages.summary: Known C1-generic tools, especially Kupka-Smale and Pugh's closing lemma, give isolated hyperbolic periodic points and density of periodic points in the nonwandering set.

proof_stages.label: Centralizer problem and C1 generic asymmetry theorem
proof_stages.summary: The central question is whether a generic diffeomorphism commutes only with its iterates, and the stated theorem of Bonatti-Crovisier-Wilkinson says this is true on every closed manifold in the C1 topology.

proof_stages.label: One-dimensional unbounded-distortion mechanism
proof_stages.summary: For commuting interval maps, the chain rule forces derivative distortions of iterates along x and g(x) to stay bounded, while a C1-generic interval map has unbounded distortion unless the two points lie on the same f-orbit, forcing g to be a local power of f.

proof_stages.label: Decomposition into wandering regions and dense invariant manifolds
proof_stages.summary: The proof uses the generic structure of the nonwandering set, including dense stable and unstable manifolds inside each interior component, to reduce the manifold to wandering-like regions or lower-dimensional invariant leaves where perturbations can be controlled.

proof_stages.label: Orbit-distinguishing C1 perturbations
proof_stages.summary: By perturbing distortion or Jacobian data along controlled orbits, the proof generically distinguishes different orbits, so any diffeomorphism g commuting with f must agree on each relevant component with some power of f.

proof_stages.label: Synchronization of local powers and residualization
proof_stages.summary: A further derivative-growth argument bounds the local exponents, after which constancy along nonperiodic orbits and a treatment of periodic points force a single global exponent, with additional category arguments upgrading density to a residual set.

proof_stages.label: Consequences, open relation spaces, and rigidity directions
proof_stages.summary: The theorem implies generic non-embeddability in flows and motivates later questions about topology of relation spaces and rigidity of smooth systems whose centralizers remain nontrivial near algebraic models.

examples.name: Near-periodic flow orbit with two strands switched
examples.note: The diagram of a nearly closed flow orbit shows why an obvious C0 strand-switching perturbation is not C1-small, motivating Pugh's more delicate C1 closing strategy.

examples.name: Rigid irrational rotation of the circle

examples.engagement_reason: she analyzes the rigid irrational circle rotation by identifying its dense orbits and centralizer, noting that only rotations commute with it
